content:
# Genesis Chapter 38 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22r–22v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_038_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_038_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 36–37

---

*Judah Goes Down — The Canaanite Wife (vv. 1–5)*

**38:1** And it came to pass at that time that Judah went down from his brothers and turned aside to a man, an Adullamite, whose name was Hirah.

**38:2** And Judah saw there the daughter of a Canaanite man whose name was Shua, and he took her and went in to her.

**38:3** And she conceived and bore a son, and he called his name Er. **38:4** And she conceived again and bore a son, and she called his name Onan. **38:5** And she yet again bore a son, and she called his name Shelah. And he was at Chezib when she bore him.

---

*Er, Onan, and the Levirate Obligation (vv. 6–11)*

**38:6** And Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, and her name was Tamar.

**38:7** But Er, Judah's firstborn, was evil in the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD put him to death.

**38:8** And Judah said to Onan, "Go in to your brother's wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her, and raise up offspring for your brother."

**38:9** But Onan knew that the offspring would not be his. So whenever he went in to his brother's wife, he would spill on the ground, so as not to give offspring to his brother. **38:10** And what he did was evil in the eyes of the LORD, and he put him to death also.

**38:11** And Judah said to Tamar his daughter-in-law, "Remain a widow in your father's house until my son Shelah grows up" — for he thought, "Lest he die also, like his brothers." So Tamar went and remained in her father's house.

---

*Judah's Wife Dies — Tamar's Stratagem (vv. 12–19)*

**38:12** And the days multiplied, and the daughter of Shua, the wife of Judah, died. And when Judah was comforted, he went up to his sheepshearers at Timnah — he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite.

**38:13** And it was told to Tamar, saying, "Behold, your father-in-law is going up to Timnah to shear his sheep."

**38:14** And she took off her widow's garments and covered herself with a veil and wrapped herself up, and sat at the entrance to Enaim, which is on the road to Timnah. For she saw that Shelah had grown up, and she had not been given to him as a wife.

**38:15** And when Judah saw her, he thought her to be a prostitute, for she had covered her face. **38:16** And he turned aside to her by the road and said, "Come now, let me come in to you" — for he did not know that she was his daughter-in-law. And she said, "What will you give me, that you may come in to me?"

**38:17** And he said, "I will send a young goat from the flock." And she said, "If you will give a pledge until you send it."

**38:18** And he said, "What pledge shall I give you?" And she said, "Your signet and your cord and your staff that is in your hand." And he gave them to her and went in to her, and she conceived by him.

**38:19** And she arose and went away, and took off her veil and put on her widow's garments.

---

*The Search for the Woman — The Pledge Unreturned (vv. 20–23)*

**38:20** And Judah sent the young goat by the hand of his friend the Adullamite, to take back the pledge from the woman's hand. But he did not find her.

**38:21** And he asked the men of her place, saying, "Where is the consecrated woman who was at Enaim, by the road?" And they said, "There has been no consecrated woman here."

**38:22** And he returned to Judah and said, "I did not find her. And the men of the place also said, 'There has been no consecrated woman here.'"

**38:23** And Judah said, "Let her keep them, lest we become a contempt. Behold, I sent this young goat, and you did not find her."

---

*Tamar's Pregnancy Revealed — The Great Confession (vv. 24–26)*

**38:24** And it came to pass, about three months later, that Judah was told, "Tamar your daughter-in-law has played the prostitute, and moreover she is pregnant by her prostitution." And Judah said, "Bring her out, and let her be burned."

**38:25** As she was being brought out, she sent to her father-in-law, saying, "By the man to whom these belong, I am pregnant." And she said, "Recognize, please — whose are these: the signet and the cord and the staff?"

**38:26** And Judah recognized them and said, "She is more righteous than I, because I did not give her to my son Shelah." And he did not know her again.

---

*The Birth of Perez and Zerah (vv. 27–30)*

**38:27** And it came to pass at the time of her giving birth that behold, there were twins in her womb.

**38:28** And it came to pass when she was giving birth that one put out a hand. And the midwife took and tied a scarlet thread on his hand, saying, "This one came out first."

**38:29** But as he drew back his hand, behold, his brother came out. And she said, "What a breach you have made for yourself!" And his name was called Perez.

**38:30** And afterward his brother came out, the one who had the scarlet thread on his hand, and his name was called Zerah.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "Went down" — vayyered Yehudah (v. 1): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS preserve "went down" for vayyered (ירד, yarad). PNEUMA renders "left his brothers and moved away," losing the descent motif entirely. The verb yarad is the same verb used for Joseph's descent to Egypt (39:1) and creates a deliberate structural parallel: Joseph descends involuntarily as a slave, Judah descends voluntarily and morally. The wordplay is the narrative hinge between the two chapters. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "went down."

**2. "Perform the duty of a brother-in-law" — yibbem (v. 8): LOGOS selected**

The three translations render the levirate verb differently: EMET has "perform the levirate duty to her" (using the Latin-derived technical term); LOGOS has "perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her" (English circumlocution preserving relational meaning); PNEUMA has "fulfill your duty as a brother-in-law to her" (dynamic equivalent). SOPHIA selects LOGOS: "perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her" — it avoids the Latin technical term "levirate" while making the institutional obligation explicit through the relational language of "brother-in-law." The Hebrew yibbem is a denominative verb from yavam ("husband's brother"), and the English rendering preserves this derivation.

**3. "He would spill on the ground" — veshichet artsah (v. 9): EMET upheld with modification**

EMET renders "he would spill on the ground" (closest to Hebrew); LOGOS renders "he would waste the seed on the ground" (adding "the seed" which is contextually implied but not in the immediate clause); PNEUMA renders "he spilled his seed on the ground" (past tense, adding "his seed"). The Hebrew veshichet artsah means literally "he destroyed/corrupted toward the ground." SOPHIA follows EMET's verb choice "spill" but adopts the iterative "would" (all three agree on the habitual aspect from the vav-consecutive construction): "he would spill on the ground." The word "seed" (zera') appears earlier in the verse ("the offspring/seed would not be his") and need not be repeated.

**4. Qedeshah — "consecrated woman" (vv. 21–22): EMET upheld**

This is the most significant translation decision in Genesis 38. The three translators diverge sharply: EMET renders qedeshah as "consecrated woman" (preserving the Hebrew root qadash, "to be holy/set apart"); LOGOS renders "cult prostitute" (traditional scholarly rendering); PNEUMA renders "cult woman" (compromise avoiding the prostitute label). The distinction between zonah (v. 15, "prostitute" — all agree) and qedeshah (vv. 21–22) is the crux. The Hebrew text deliberately uses two different words: the narrator and Judah's perception use zonah (common commercial prostitute); Hirah the Adullamite uses qedeshah when asking publicly. The LXX collapses this distinction, using pornē for both — a significant flattening of the narrative texture.

SOPHIA rules with EMET: "consecrated woman." The reasoning: (1) The Hebrew root qadash means "holy, set apart, consecrated" — this is what the word says. (2) The scholarly consensus on "sacred prostitution" in the ancient Near East has shifted significantly; the evidence for institutionalized cult prostitution is increasingly contested. (3) "Cult prostitute" imports an interpretation the Hebrew does not require. (4) "Consecrated woman" preserves the precise lexical contrast the narrator has crafted — Judah thinks "prostitute" (zonah), Hirah asks for a "consecrated woman" (qedeshah), using a more elevated and socially dignified term. Whether this reflects Hirah's delicacy, a different social category, or a genuine cultic association is left open by the translation, as the Hebrew leaves it open.

**5. "Recognize, please" — hakker-na (v. 25): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS both render hakker-na as "Recognize, please" — preserving the exact verbal echo with Genesis 37:32, where the brothers used the identical phrase when presenting Joseph's bloody coat to Jacob. PNEUMA renders "Please look carefully," which captures the sense but destroys the echo. The verbal link between 37:32 and 38:25 is one of the most precisely crafted literary devices in the Joseph cycle: the same imperative (Pi'el of nakar) plus the particle na. Judah participated in the deception of Jacob with false evidence and the words "recognize, please"; now Tamar confronts Judah with true evidence and the same words. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "Recognize, please."

**6. "A contempt" — lavuz (v. 23): EMET upheld**

EMET renders pen nihyeh lavuz as "lest we become a contempt." LOGOS and PNEUMA render "lest we become a laughingstock" / "otherwise we will become a laughingstock." The Hebrew lavuz (from buz, "contempt, scorn") carries more weight than "laughingstock" — it denotes social contempt, not mere ridicule. Judah fears being shamed as one who could not retrieve his own pledge items, exposing the whole transaction. SOPHIA follows EMET: "lest we become a contempt" — preserving the stronger social weight of the Hebrew.

**7. "The days multiplied" — vayirbu hayyamim (v. 12): EMET upheld**

EMET preserves the unusual Hebrew idiom "the days multiplied." LOGOS renders dynamically as "in the course of time"; PNEUMA as "Time passed." The Hebrew vayirbu hayyamim is itself an unusual temporal expression — not the standard "after many days" — and its specific diction suggests an accumulation of time pressing Tamar's situation toward urgency. SOPHIA follows EMET's literal rendering: "the days multiplied."

**8. "She is more righteous than I" — tsadeqah mimmeni (v. 26): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators render Judah's confession identically: "She is more righteous than I." The comparative structure is clear and the meaning is both moral and forensic. The LXX's dedikaiōtai ("has been justified") shifts to a divine passive, but the MT's active Qal — Judah's own direct declaration — is followed.

**9. "What a breach you have made for yourself!" (v. 29): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders "What a breach you have broken forth" (closer to the Hebrew paratsah wordplay); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "What a breach you have made for yourself!" — which captures the force while being more natural English. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "What a breach you have made for yourself!"

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 36–37)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 38:5 | כְזִיב (Chezib) | Χασβι (Chasbi) | Follow MT: "Chezib" |
| 38:8 | וְיַבֵּם אֹתָהּ (yibbem — levirate duty) | συνοίκησον ("dwell with") | Follow MT: "perform the duty of a brother-in-law" — preserving the specific institutional term |
| 38:14 | בְּפֶתַח עֵינַיִם (petach enayim) | ἐπὶ τῆς πύλης Αιναν ("at the gate of Ainan") | Follow MT syntax: "entrance to Enaim" — place name reading confirmed by LXX |
| 38:15 | לְזוֹנָה (zonah — prostitute) | πόρνη (prostitute) | Follow MT: "prostitute" — consistent across traditions |
| 38:18 | חֹתָמְךָ וּפְתִילֶךָ וּמַטְּךָ (signet, cord, staff) | δακτύλιον, ὁρμίσκον, ῥάβδον (ring, necklace, staff) | Follow MT: "signet and cord and staff" — LXX interprets the objects differently |
| 38:21 | הַקְּדֵשָׁה (qedeshah) | πόρνη (pornē — prostitute) | Follow MT: "consecrated woman" — LXX collapses the zonah/qedeshah distinction |
| 38:25 | הַכֶּר-נָא (hakker-na) | ἐπίγνωθι (recognize) | Follow MT: "Recognize, please" — echo with 37:32 preserved in both traditions |
| 38:26 | צָדְקָה מִמֶּנִּי (she is more righteous than I) | δεδικαίωται Θαμαρ ἢ ἐγώ (Tamar has been justified more than I) | Follow MT: direct active declaration, not divine passive |
| 38:29 | פָּרֶץ (Perez) | Φαρες (Phares) | Follow MT form: "Perez" — English convention |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
